coredata.dc:title: Coupling nano-redox carbon dots with nontraditional AIE-polysaccharides on a smart fabric for healthcare sensing systems
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Xianzhu
Yan, Kun
Yang, Chenguang
Wang, Dong
coredata.dc:description: Creating bio-based fluorescent probes with visual-sensing abilities and anti-infection performance is highly desirable but remains challenging. Herein, a multifunctional dual-responsive biopolymeric nanocoating has been designed with synergistically enhanced fluorescent and antibacterial properties that simply implemented by the combination of tannic acid rooted carbon dots (TN, redox-sensitive) and alginate-zinc complex (SA-Zn, ionic-sensitive) possessing the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics. The mussel-inspired catechol groups and alginate-metal aggregates afford great film-forming abilities on diverse substrates while the entrapped TN enable manipulation of the redox-dependent fluorescent and antibacterial properties via generating reactive free radicals (ROS). The concept of facile pre-sensing and treatment towards in-home sensing application has been perfectly realized on a commercial fabric wipe, demonstrating excellent detection capabilities for biological molecules (e.g., H2O2 and metal cations) and synergistically enhanced antibacterial performance. Therefore, bridging the nano-redox carbon dots with nontraditional luminescent polysaccharides can provide enormous potential into advanced fluorescent materials for emerging healthcare applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tannic acid
Alginate
AIE
Carbon dots
Antibacterial

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Green and Sustainable Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of TN/SA fluorescent inks and coatings on the fabric substrate
Preparation of the cation crosslinked nanocoating
Investigation of the redox- and metal-mediated fluorescent activities
Constructing advanced fluorescent materials from nano-redox carbon dots and AIE polysaccharides
Synthesis and characterizations of the smart fluorescent fabrics
Redox-sensitive fluorescent abilities of TN/SA nanocoating
Metal-mediated fluorescent activities and highly sensing performance

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by financial support of National Natural Science Foundation (12205225). We thank the “Educational Commission of Hubei Province” and “Wuhan Engineering Technology Research Center for Advanced Fibers” providing partial support of this project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bridging nano-redox carbon dots with nontraditional AIE polymers to construct a smart fabric platform for healthcare sensing systems. (a) Schematically illustrates the concept of designing the multifunctional materials with synergistically enhanced fluorescent and antibacterial activities. (b) Sequential modifications and reconstruction of the fabrics by using TN/SA inks with a simple infiltration, and then dried at room temperature; the Zn ions were used to further improve the film properties via ions exchanging and in-situ cross-linking. (c) Hypothesis and requirements of the smart wipes for healthcare sensing systems. (d) Fluorescent images indicate the abilities and potential applications of smart wipes for surfactant contaminations’ fast removal and visual detection in daily life.

Structural analysis and characterizations. (a) TEM and size distribution of the tannic acid rooted nanoparticles (TN). (b) Optical images illustrated the TN can be uniformly dispersed in an alginate solution as the fluorescent inks and coating. (c) UV–vis measurement demonstrates the homogeneous mixing feature and fluorescent activities of TN/SA mixtures. (d) Optical, (e) surface-structural (SEM), (f) chemical (FTIR), (g) mechanical, and (h) crystalline (XRD) evidences to reveal the micro-structural evolutions and molecular interactions formed between each component of the reconstructed smart fabrics.

Investigation of the redox-mediated optical performance of TN/SA fluorescent inks. (a) Schematically illustrates the fluorescent properties are dependent on the oxidation/reduction states of TN. (b, c) Two model redox-sensitive dyes were used to confirm the antioxidant activities of TN (the ABTS + radical solution was generated by an electrochemical oxidation method, 4 V, 5 min, 0.1 M PBS buffer). (d) Optical images of the fluorescent patterns at different states and (e) PL measurements were carried out to reveal the redox-sensitive and stabilities of the nanocoating. (f) Comparable studies of the redox-dependent PL intensities of the films with different compositions. (g) Schematically illustrated the proposed mechanisms of redox-sensitive fluorescent activities. PL curves and the corresponding peak intensities as a function of the molecule concentrations for (h) H2O2 and (i) ascorbic acid to real the fluorescent properties and sensing performance.

Using the ionic crosslinks induced AIE effects to confer enhanced fluorescent properties and sensing abilities. (a) Schematically illustration of the fundamental mechanisms of the ionic crosslinks induced AIE effects based on the reversible metal-polymer coordination interactions. PL curves of (b) SA and (c) TN/SA polymeric coatings treated with different metal salt concentrations. (d) Optical images and (e) peak intensities of the SA and TN/SA coatings to show the fluorescent improvements after being treated with different Zn concentrations. (f) The change in fluorescence intensity (ΔF) for various cation crosslinked fabrics against the fluorescence (F0) of the bare substrate (excitation wavelength, λex = 340 nm; emission wavelength, λem = 420 nm); inserts show the fluorescence responses to various metal cations. PL curves of (g) bare fabric and (h) TN/SA/Zn samples versus different Fe concentrations. (i) Comparable studies of the fluorescent properties of different fabrics and their sensing performances against Fe. (j) Potential applications for rapid optical detection and removal of the surface contaminations on an apple.

Comprehensive studies of the redox-/metal- mediated synergistic antibacterial performance. (a) Enlist the possible antibacterial mechanisms and multi-stimulus advantages. (b) Surface morphologies of the fabrics reveal the redox-dependent antibacterial and adhesion activities. Inhibition zone tests to determine the antibacterial performance of (c) TN/SA and (d) TN/SA/Zn coatings against the bacteria of S.aureus and E. coli at nature- and UV-light. (e) The summary of zone diameters indicates the redox-mediated antibacterial activities of TN/SA. (f) The proposed mechanisms of metal-/redox-mediated synergistic antibacterial properties. (g) Inhibition zone diameters of TN/SA coatings after loaded with different concentrations of metal salts.

head.ce:title: Coupling nano-redox carbon dots with nontraditional AIE-polysaccharides on a smart fabric for healthcare sensing systems

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application, Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials & Application, Key Laboratory of Textile Fiber & Product, Ministry of Education, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application
Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials & Application
Key Laboratory of Textile Fiber & Product

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Advanced Textile Materials & Application, Hubei International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Intelligent Textile Materials & Application, Key Laboratory of Textile Fiber & Product, Ministry of Education, Wuhan Textile University, Wuhan 430200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bridging the nano-redox carbon dots with nontraditional luminescent polysaccharides can provide enormous potential into advanced fluorescent materials for emerging healthcare applications.

Creating bio-based fluorescent probes with visual-sensing abilities and anti-infection performance is highly desirable but remains challenging. Herein, a multifunctional dual-responsive biopolymeric nanocoating has been designed with synergistically enhanced fluorescent and antibacterial properties that simply implemented by the combination of tannic acid rooted carbon dots (TN, redox-sensitive) and alginate-zinc complex (SA-Zn, ionic-sensitive) possessing the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics. The mussel-inspired catechol groups and alginate-metal aggregates afford great film-forming abilities on diverse substrates while the entrapped TN enable manipulation of the redox-dependent fluorescent and antibacterial properties via generating reactive free radicals (ROS). The concept of facile pre-sensing and treatment towards in-home sensing application has been perfectly realized on a commercial fabric wipe, demonstrating excellent detection capabilities for biological molecules (e.g., H2O2 and metal cations) and synergistically enhanced antibacterial performance. Therefore, bridging the nano-redox carbon dots with nontraditional luminescent polysaccharides can provide enormous potential into advanced fluorescent materials for emerging healthcare applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The global outbreak of bacterial infections has becoming one of the leading causes of death in recent decades and creating awareness to the urgent need for antibacterial materials. Since the ongoing worldwide bacterial contamination has affected our life and led to a global economic stagnation, it is highly important to decline the morbidities and prevent human beings from bacterial infections [1]. To avoid antibiotic resistance, therefore, developing a newly home-use multifunctional antibacterial reagent with the ability to visually detect potential contaminations in advance is of great significance to reduce the morbidities caused by bacterial infection in daily life [2,3].

For anti-infection abilities, transition nanometals possessing several applicable features such as inexpensive, easily obtained, and combined with other therapeutics, have often been recognized as effective inorganic antibacterial reagents for various biomedical fields [4,5]. However, the weaknesses of severely restricted dosages and unstainable behaviors have limited their widespread applications. Recently, the reactive oxygen species (ROS) as another alternative strategy has captured more attention for biomedical use because of which can induce the damage and kill the bacteria [6–9]. Numerous efforts have been devoted to preparing the materials with excellent capabilities to generate ROS sustainably. Nonetheless, the short-life characteristics made ROS take effect slowly and resulted in a limited antibacterial ability. Considering the above descriptions, it enlightened us a creative idea to combine these two antibacterial reagents together as a newly sustainable and effective antibacterial reagents[10], such as the metals were used for high-efficient sterilization at the initial state while the regenerative ROS served as the sustainable reagents.

Towards sensing requirements, various fluorescent materials such as quantum dots[11], carbon dots[12], metal nanoclusters[13], and metal–organic frameworks[14], etc. possessing vast advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, real-time and simple operation, have been extensively investigated. Among them the carbon dots, as a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with a size below 20 nm, have attracted broad research interest due to their diverse physicochemical properties and favorable attributes like good biocompatibility, intrinsic fluorescence properties, abundant functional groups (e.g., amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl), high stability, and electron mobility[15,16]. In recent decades, increasing interests have been attracted to developing a high-performance fluorescent material, especially for visual/rapid detection of biological contaminations without using complex instruments in daily life[17].

Since the new concept called ‘‘aggregation-induced emission (AIE)’’ was first proposed by Tang et al in 2001, the luminescent materials have seized wide attentions and becoming a research hot point for detecting daily contaminations[18–20]. Unlike conventional aggregation-caused quench (ACQ) dyes, AIE luminogens show weak or negligible emission in dilute solution but emit intensely in the aggregate or solid state and captured much attention as an alternative for traditional fluorescent sensing molecules. However, the inherent toxicity, time-consuming synthesis processes, tedious purification, high cost, limited emission efficiency, and non-degradability have strongly limited their biomedical applications. Recently, natural abundant polysaccharides have been widely explored because of their favorable features, such as biodegradable, less cost, hydrophilic, biocompatible, and non-toxic making it a favorable feedstock to produce fluorescent materials[21]. Interestingly, the crosslinked polysaccharides are reported to show biological fluorescence and emission sensitivity to transition metal ions (e.g., Ca2+ and Zn2+)[22]. For instance, the Ca2+ doped sodium alginate complexes show enhanced blue fluorescence based on the clustering of oxygen atoms and carboxyl groups, and conformation rigidification[23]. In addition, the Zn2+ coordinated polymer films displayed an enhanced blue fluorescence, and the excitation/concentration-dependent characteristics could be explained by an AIE mechanism[24,25]. Specifically, the alginate exhibits AIE characteristics, and its fluorescent properties could be further enhanced/manipulated by the metal cations[26]. Therefore, these non-traditional luminescent materials put new insight into various functional materials such as toxic metal cations and anions sensing, security printing, bioimaging and other environmental purposes[27,28].

Considering of above descriptions, in this study, a dual-responsive fluorescent nanocoating probe has been firstly designed via a perfect combination of the redox-sensitive carbon dots and metal-sensitive AIE polymers. Specifically, the synergistic improved optical and antibacterial properties that simply implemented from the tannic acid rooted carbon dots (TN, redox-sensitive) and alginate-zinc complex (SA-Zn, ionic-sensitive) possessing the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics. Additionally, the high sensing abilities of biological species and sustainable antibacterial properties of the smart wipes modified with high-performance fluorescent nanocoating have been comprehensively investigated. Therefore, this versatile fluorescent nanocoating can spur new insight into advanced fluorescent materials for emerging biomedical applications.

The following materials were purchased from Aladdin: tannic acid (TA), sodium hydroxide (NAOH), ethanol (C2H5OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium alginate (SA), white vitriol (ZnSO4), calcium chloride (CaCl2), silver nitrate (AgNO3), ferric chloride (FeCl3), nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2), copper sulfate (CuSO4), Chromic sulfate (Cr2(SO4)3), Cobalt sulfate (CoSO4), 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS), L-Ascorbic acid (ASC) and Luria–Bertani (LB) medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of TN/SA fluorescent inks and coatings on the fabric substrate
ce:section.ce:para: The tannic acid (TA) rooted carbon quantum dots (TN) were firstly prepared according to a previously reported method as follows[29]: (1) add 0.1 g tannic acid, 5 ml anhydrous ethanol and 20 mg sodium hydroxide into a beaker with constantly magnetic stirring and ultrasonic for 30 min. Next, 2 ml hydrogen peroxide solution (30 %) has been added to the above solution and heat/reflux at 80℃ for 2 h. Finally, centrifuge the supernatant at 6000 rpm for 10 min and freeze-dry to obtain the TN powders. The fluorescent TN/SA mixtures were prepared by dispersing 20 mg TN and 0.5 g sodium alginate powders in 50 ml distilled water with magnetic stirring at room temperature for 2 h to achieve a homogeneous state. After completely mixed, the immersed the fabric substrates into above fluorescent inks for 1 h and then condensed vacuum dried at 25 ◦C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the cation crosslinked nanocoating
ce:section.ce:para: The cation crosslinking processes were conducted as follows. For single cation crosslinking, the condensed vacuum dried films were cut into the same size (1.5 cm * 1.5 cm * 75.6 μm) and then soaked in a Zn2+ solution for 6 h to allow the fabric fully swelled, the ions adequate exchange, crosslink with polymer chains and reach an equilibrium state (note: the other cations are using the same method). After the cations loaded, the fabrics were recollected and gently rinsed with distilled water for three times before further measurements. Various metal concentrations of Zn have been used such as 0.1 M, 0.5 M and 1 M, and the resulted fabrics were coded as TN/SA/Zn0.1, TN/SA/Zn0.5 and TN/SA/Zn1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the redox- and metal-mediated fluorescent activities
ce:section.ce:para: The TN/SA coatings at reductive state (coded TNRED/SA) and oxidative state (coded TNOX/SA) were prepared by soaking the fabrics in an ascorbic acid solution (300 mM) or hydrogen peroxide solution (10 %) for 30 min, respectively.

The radical scavenging assay was conducted as follows: the ABTS (1 mM) were converted to its radical cation form (ABTS+ ) by an electrochemical oxidation method (4 V, 5 min, 0.1 M PBS buffer)[30]. Put the fabrics into the cuvettes containing 3 ml DPPH (100 μM) and ABTS (1 mM) solutions. Their scavenging activities were evaluated by detecting the decrease of absorbance at 515 nm and 424 nm, respectively. To investigate the ionic crosslinks induced AIE effects, the fabrics modified with TN/SA nanocoating were immersed into different metal ion solutions (zinc, iron, nickel, copper, silver, chromium and cobalt ions) with the same concentration of 0.1 M for 5 min. The Zinc loaded fabric has been chosen to test the sensing performance against different concentrations of iron ions.

The antibacterial activities of the fluorescent fabrics were determined by using a typical disc-diffusion technique. The glass petri-dishes (diameter, 90 mm) were filled with Luri-Bertain agar and then inoculated separately with two bacterial strains of S. aureus and E. coli with a bacterial concentration of 108 ∼ 109 colony-forming unite cfu*ml−1. The pre-prepared fabrics with varied compositions were placed promptly on the agar surface. The bare fabric sample fabricated without polymer coating was provided as a control. The inhibition zones were determined after 24 h of incubation at 37 ℃ and the zone diameter of each sample was calculated from the obtained image by using the nano-measure software.

The fluorescent images of the fabric samples were collected by using a hand-held ultraviolet lamp and irradiating at the excitation wavelength (λex) of 365 nm. Optical images of the hydrogels were collected by using a digital camera. The microstructure observations were performed by using a scanning electron microscope (SEM; VEGA3 LMU, TESCAN). Molecular interactions were revealed by using a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Vertex70, Bruker) in the wavenumber of 400 ∼ 4000 cm−1. Mechanical properties of the cylindrical shaped gel pellets were performed by using a universal testing machine (CMT6350, Shenzhen SANS, China) under the stress model at the rate of 1 mm/min. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were measured on a JEM-2010FEF (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) microscope. Spectrophotometric measurements were carried out using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Hitachi, Japan). The crystal structure was revealed by using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, PANalytical X’Pert Pro) under an operating voltage of 40 kV and a current of 20 mA. Photoluminescence emission (PL) and excitation (PLE) spectra were measured by using an EDX1800B fluorescence spectrophotometer with a scan rate of 2000 nm/min and resolution of 0.2 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Constructing advanced fluorescent materials from nano-redox carbon dots and AIE polysaccharides
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescent materials with multifunctional applications have attained increasing interest in recent decades. There is of great interest to develop a high-performance fluorescent material especially for the rapid and visual detection of surfactant contaminations without using complex instruments in daily life[31,32]. Therefore, as shown in Fig. 1
a, this study aims to create a versatile fluorescent nanocoating by coupling the redox-sensitive carbon dots with nontraditional AIE polymers for advanced healthcare materials. Towards potential biomedical applications, initially, two naturally abundant biopolymers: tannic acid/alginate (TA/SA) have been chosen to prepare the fluorescent inks. Specifically, the tannic acid rooted nanoparticles (TN) with abundant interfacial catechol groups have been integrated that not only to provide great fluorescent properties but also afford redox sensitive abilities to the nanocoating (e.g., generate ROS). Based on the excellent film-forming capabilities and ionic sensitive aggregation induced emission (AIE) characteristic, additionally, the typical alginate-zinc (SA-Zn) was chosen to generate the coating matrix that evenly entrapped TN on diverse substrates.

To demonstrate the concept of applications for healthcare use, a commercial cellulose-based wet wipe (e.g., spunlace nonwoven fabric) has been used as the substrate. As schematically shown in Fig. 1 b, the preparation procedures of the smart wipes can be divided into two parts. Firstly, the homogeneous TN/SA inks were integrated into the fabric via an infiltration method and then vaccuum dried at room temperature for 12 h to obtain a dense fluorescent nanocoating. Secondly, the obtained fabrics were soaked in a coagulating bath (0.1 M ZnCl2, 6 h) that allow the partial rehydration of the nanocoating, spontaneous permeation of metal ions, and in-situ crosslinking with the polymer networks. Finally, the TN were expected to be evenly trapped and immobilized on the fabric surface as the metal-polymer complexation formation. It needs to note that, the mussel-inspired functional groups on the TN surface and the great film-forming feature of SA promise such fluorescent coatings to be prepared on diverse substrates such as glasses, polymers and stainless steel surfaces ( Fig. S1
) via the interfacial hydrogen bonds and/or mussel-inspired catechol-metal interactions[33,34]. Towards the potential healthcare sensing applications, as shown in Fig. 1 c, preventing our families away from in-home surfactant contaminations, complex requirements have been raised such as the high-sensitivity, visual sense and facile operation for pre-detecting and rapid cleaning of harmful microorganisms (e.g., bacterial, virus and other toxic molecules). The nanocoating were composted of two natural abundant biopolymers (e.g., tannic acid, alginate) and traditional metal cation (Zn2+), and which have been reported to have good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity at a reasonable dose in recent literatures[35]. The images in Fig. 1 d indicate the possibilities of patterning fluorescent nanocoating onto either a hard or soft substrate (e.g., glass or fabric) and showing great potential applications for healthcare usage (e.g., detecting toxic residues).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterizations of the smart fluorescent fabrics
ce:section.ce:para: The tannic acid rooted carbon dots were first prepared according to a previously reported method[29]. Briefly, the TA chains were able to form the corresponding nanoparticles (TN, ∼3 nm diameter, Fig. 2a) through a hydroxyl radical-induced degradation process in the presence of H2O2 (30 % w/w) and NaOH (1 M). Next, the TN with blue fluorescent appearances were evenly dispersed in 1 w/v % SA solution, which could be prepared as a uniform nanocomposite film after drying at 25℃for 12 h (Fig. 2 b). These results could be further confirmed by an obvious blue-shift of the characteristic adsorption peak at 280 nm (TA) was changed to 265 nm (for TN and TN/SA samples) (Fig. 2c). The details in preparations and zeta potential of TN are provided in Fig. S2 . The bright and fluorescent images displayed in Fig. 2d were used to confirm the optical changes of the fabrics coated with TN/SA fluorescent inks and further cross-linked by Zn2+. It can be seen that the fluorescent inks were able to confer relatively strong fluorescent properties to the fabrics, which could be further improved by the formation of metal-polymer complexations. These results are well agreed with other reports that the ionic crosslinks induced aggregation of alginates with enhanced fluorescent intensities (e.g., AIE effects) via strong coordination interactions[36,37].

Fig. 2e indicates the fabrics were becoming rougher and more compact after being modified with different concentrations of Zn ions (e.g., 0.1 M, 0.5 M, and 1 M), and the corresponding samples were coded as TN/SA/Zn0.1, TN/SA/Zn0.5 and TN/SA/Zn1, respectively. The intermolecular interactions have been revealed by the FTIR measurements. As shown in Fig. 2f, the characteristic bands of both controls were observed in the TN/SA/Zn composite samples. All the samples show strong bands at ∼ 1315 cm−1 and ∼ 1420 cm−1(-C-H bending vibration), 2880 cm−1 (-C-H stretching vibration), ∼3274 cm−1 (-O-H stretching vibration) which are characteristic peaks of bare fabric substrate and TN/SA[38]. Additionally, the new bands at 1710 cm−1, 1450 cm−1, 1080 cm−1 correspond to C = O, C-OH, and C-O-C stretching vibration indicating the existence of TN in the nanocomposites[39]. The characteristic bands at 1622 cm−1, 1543 cm−1 and 1456 cm−1 reflected the stretching vibrations of the benzene skeleton and the ∼ 1594 cm−1 and ∼ 1415 cm−1 are attributed to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups[40]. After crosslinking with Zn, the stretching vibration at ∼ 1204 cm−1 was weakened as evidenced by the complex formation between phenolic groups and Zn. Similarly, the enhanced intensity of the C-OH stretching peak (located at ∼ 1450 cm−1) indicated the hydroxyl groups coordinated with Zn ions. The zinc was expected to chelate with two carboxylic groups of SA chains via electrostatic bridges, which led to the formation of a stiffened metal-polymer complexation.

Fig. 2g provides the mechanical evidences for the TN/SA/Zn complex’s successful formation. The mechanical strength of the bare fabrics can be slightly improved by soaking in a Zn2+ containing bath while significantly enhanced with the integration of TN/SA/Zn networks formation[41]. These results indicated the polymer coating could not only conferred optical functionalities to the fabric but also played an important role in the mechanical framework. The crystalline structural changes have been revealed by X-ray diffraction in Fig. 2h. The relative sharp peak at 23° was attributed to a semi-amorphous state of the bare fabric. A slightly blue-shift and enhanced intensity of the fabric could be observed after modification with TN/SA nano-coating. These could be assigned to the more hydrogen bonds formed within the dense fabric after a drying process. The integration of metal cations was found to effectively influence the crystalline structure, as the characteristic peak intensities decreased with the increase of metal concentrations. These results indicated that strong coordination interactions were formed between the polymers and metal cations. The new peaks appeared at 18°, 21°, 26.5°, 29° and 37° could be assigned to the excessive addition of Zn and resulted in a hexagonal phase of ZnO with wurtzite faces for the samples of TN/SA/Zn0.5 and TN/SA/Zn1. The loading efficiency of each component on the fabric (e.g., SA/TN/Zn) and the water uptake abilities have been tested by using a weighting method at each step. The results are shown in Fig. S3. Because of the Zn crosslinked sample showed great stabilities and no observed TN were released out from the nano-coatings. The results suggested the composite fabric possessed good water uptake ability, achieving to a considerable loading efficiency of 88.09 %, 1.53 %, 1.68 %, and 8.7 % for the H2O, TN, SA and Zn/ZnO metals, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Redox-sensitive fluorescent abilities of TN/SA nanocoating
ce:section.ce:para: The underlying mechanisms of the antioxidant and electron-donating abilities of TN are schematically shown in Fig. 3 a. Since the abundant catechol groups on the TA and/or TN surfaces, they have been reported to be an efficient antioxidant for scavenging activities via donating electrons to quench oxidative free radicals and mediate the fluorescent activities via QH/Q or Q*/Q pathways[30]. The initial evidences were provided by the XPS spectra of TN/SA coated fabrics before and after ascorbate treatment. The ascorbate treated (i.e., reduced) fabric has an increased C-O peak at about ∼ 532 eV and a decreased C = O peak at about ∼ 531 eV compared to the oxidized sample (Fig. S4). This result is consistent with an ascorbate-mediated reduction of oxidized quinone moieties to catechol moieties. To reveal above hypothesis, the radical scavenging assays of DPPH and ABTS+ have been tested as shown in Fig. 3b and 3c, respectively. As expected, the electron-donating abilities of TN for the anti-oxidation radical scavenging activity were highly dependent on the TN redox states and only the TNRED/SA samples were able to degrade two colorful solutions to transparent as their absorbance values rapidly decreased approximately to 0 within 40 s. However, no changes can be seen from the controls such as the bare fabric, TN and TNOX/SA samples, indicating the entrapped TN has great redox state-dependent radical scavenging activities within the coating matrix (Details for controls were provided in Fig. S5 ).

Fluorescent images in Fig. 3d indicated the TN could be used to confer fluorescent properties to the fabrics with pre-designed patterning structures and exhibited a similar redox-dependent fluorescent behavior with or without integration of SA polymer matrix. However, as compared to the pure TN sample, the fluorescent patterns of TN/SA displayed a relatively higher resolution and fluorescent intensity suggesting the TN could be substantially trapped on the fabric substrate via SA polymeric coating. To reveal the stabilities of the nano-coatings, the fabrics coated with varied components have been immersed into a cuvette (2 ml H2O, 24 h, R.T.) for in-situ monitoring of the release behavior of TN from each sample. The time-dependent UV curves of the solutions and fluorescent intensities changes of the soaked fabrics are provided in Figs. S6 and S7, respectively. It can be seen that the peak absorbances of the solutions at about 280 nm were progressively increased for the pure TN and TN/SA samples, indicating partially of TN were released out to the solutions. However, no obvious changes could be found from the TN/SA/Zn sample. These results suggested the TN were relatively stable in the SA matrix, and which can be well fixed by using the Zn2+ treatments. To further quantitative studies of the fluorescent properties of TN and TN/SA, the photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) measurement has been employed and their PL curves are shown in Fig. 3e and Fig. S8 , respectively. Fig. 3f has summarized the fluorescence intensity change (ΔF) for various samples against the fluorescence (F0) of bare fabric (λex = 340 nm). The relative lower fluorescent intensities of pure TN samples may be explained by the loss of amount from the fabric surface during the treatment processes. The proposed mechanisms for redox-sensitive fluorescent properties are schematically illustrated in Fig. 3g. Due to the abundant catechol groups on the TN surface, under excitation conditions, the TN carbon dots would donate the electrons to surfactant QH groups and then change to Q, resulting in a lower emission efficiency (i.e., lower fluorescent intensity) at the TNOX state. On the contrary, most of the Q groups cannot accept the electrons from the TN carbon dots and leading to a higher emission efficiency (i.e., higher fluorescent intensity) at the TNRED state[42].

Therefore, these redox-sensitive abilities provide great possibilities for the visual detection of biological molecules with oxidative and/or reductive properties. The commonly used oxidative reagent of H2O2 and reductive reagents of ascorbic acid have been employed, and the results were shown in Fig. 3h and 3 g. It can be seen that the PL intensities at 388 nm progressively decreased as the increased concentrations of H2O2 from 0 % to 0.5 %, achieving a linear relationship with the R2 ∼ 0.994. As expected, for ascorbic acid, the PL intensities were progressively increased as the decreased concentrations, resulting in a linear relationship with the R2 ∼ 0.987 (e.g., 0 mM to 60 mM). These results indicate that fluorescent intensities are highly sensitive to the redox molecules and possess huge potential applications for healthcare systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal-mediated fluorescent activities and highly sensing performance
ce:section.ce:para: The AIE materials show weak or negligible emission in dilute solution but emit intensely in the aggregate or solid state and captured much attention as an alternative for traditional fluorescent sensing molecules. The crosslinked biopolymers are reported to show enhanced blue fluorescence and emission sensitivity to transition metal ions (e.g., Ca2+ and Zn2+) based on the clustering of oxygen atoms and carboxyl groups, and conformation rigidification[43]. Therefore, the typical Zn2+ has been chosen to crosslink with SA that not only to manipulate the fluorescent properties but also to confer great antibacterial activities. As schematically illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescent intensities could be regulated by the metal cations (i.e., Zn2+) as the metal-polymer complexation formed via the specific “egg-box” structure. The ionic crosslinks induced aggregation of SA chains was expected to result in an enhanced fluorescent intensity (e.g., known as the AIE effects), which could be erased by removing metal cations from the polymer network to an un-crosslink state.

Interestingly, both of the PL curves in Fig. 4b and 4c were found to show a similar trend with their peak intensities increased as the polymer coating and metal cations induced cross-links formation, achieving the maximum PL intensity at a moderate concentration of 0.1 M. When further increased to 1 M, the PL intensities were decreased, suggesting the excessive crosslinks would limit the AIE effects. The PL intensities of TN/SA were much higher than SA indicating they are highly sensitive to the metal cations in the presence of TN. Fig. 4d displayed the optical and fluorescent images of the fabrics prepared with various compositions. Their peak intensity improvements against the bare fabric sample have been calculated in Fig. 4e. The integration of TN was found to significantly improve the fluorescent intensity with about 2.6 times higher than the SA coatings irrespective of the Zn concentrations, indicating that TN contributes most to the synergistic effects of their fluorescent properties.

To test the cation-responsive abilities, the optical appearances of the TN/SA coated fabrics treated with various metal cations and their corresponding change ratios (ΔF/F0) in fluorescence intensities are displayed in Fig. 4f. Interestingly, only the Zn2+ cross-linked fabric display enhanced fluorescent properties while the others exhibit a contrary trend that turns into quenching states in response to various cations including Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Cr2+ and Co2+ (Their PL curves are provided in Fig. S9 ). These results could be assigned to an aggregation-caused quenching effect (ACQ). Considering great biological functions and high quenching degree, Fe3+ ions were chosen as the model cations to investigate the fluorescent sensing performance. The PL curves of bare fabrics and TN/SA/Zn have been summarized in Fig. 4g and h, respectively. The results of the controls of TN and SA/Zn are provided in Fig. S10 . It can be seen that all the fabrics showed metal-dependent quenching dynamics with the peak intensities highly sensitive to Fe3+. Therefore, it is conceivable that the fluorescent activities of TN and typical SA-Zn2+ complexes are somehow functionality dependent within the coating networks. The TN/SA/Zn that combined above two fluorescent units (e.g., Carbon dots and AIE polymers) have possessed a significantly high fluorescent intensity and high-sensitive activity to the Fe3+ (Fig. 4i), achieving improvements of 49.6, 12.4, 36.5 times to that of the SA/Zn, TN and bare fabric samples, respectively. Thus, the significant change of fluorescent intensities to an ultra-low concentration of surfactant contaminations (e.g., Fe3+, 0.1 mM) provides a great potential for visual detection without a complex instrument. The images displayed in Fig. 4j demonstrate the smart fabrics could be fixed on the fingers and served as the multiple sensing units. Once attaching to the apple with a residual of Fe3+ (e.g., 0.1 mM) for 1 min, the fluorescent intensities of the contacting areas were observed to rapidly decreased while no changes were formed to the other sensing units. These results indicate the great potential applications for rapid and visual detection of surfactant contaminations.

The synergistic antibacterial activity and mechanism have been revealed in Fig. 5 . As schematically illustrated in Fig. 5a, the catechol groups on TN could be externally stimulated (e.g., chemical, electro-chemical and enzymatic) to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the presence of O2, which has been widely used for sustainable anti-infection in biomedical fields. Additionally, the metal cations at appropriate dosages have commonly been recognized as excellent inorganic antibacterial reagents. However, the strong antibacterial performance was seemed unsustainable due to the limited metal sources, and which could only be fueled by chemical methods. Therefore, it is highly desirable to acquire a complementary and sustainable anti-infection solution via the combination of above two systems[44]. Fig. 5b displayed the micro-structural observations of the fabrics after co-cultured with E. coli at 37℃ for 24 h. It can be seen that the bare fabric and TNOX/SA samples both showed weak antibacterial performance with massive of live E. coli attaching to the fiber surfaces. Only some bacteria debris could be seen from TN/SA suggesting a considerable antibacterial activity, and which could be assigned to a certain amount of ROS formation. Interestingly, the TNOX/SA shows a clean surface with no live/dead bacteria attached, indicating the strong antibacterial and anti-adhesion capabilities[9].

The antibacterial performance has been further confirmed by using an agar-plate diffusion method. As shown in Fig. 5c and 5d, only the TNRED/SA samples show a small inhibition zone around the films, suggesting the ROS possesses considerable antibacterial properties against to the bacteria of S.aureu and E.coli. Fig. 5e compares the antibacterial activities of the fabrics modified with TN/SA coating and varied concentrations of metal cations. Obvious antibacterial zones could be found around the metal containing samples and showed an uptrend with the cation concentrations. These results could be mainly explained by a concentration-driven rapid diffusion of metal cation and then reacting/killing the bacteria. A proposed synergistic enhanced antibacterial mechanism has been schematically shown in Fig. 5f. The inhibition antibacterial zones diameters have been calculated and summarized in Fig. 5g. Besides the generation of ROS, the redox activities of catechol groups were expected to assist the reduction of metal ions and promote the antibacterial performance[45]. Therefore, a synergistic enhanced and sustainable antibacterial system could be facilely prepared by coupling the nano-redox carbon dots with the specific metal-polymer complexations. These phenomenons are well consistent with the results that metal cations were able to effectively improve the antibacterial performance of the TN/SA/Zn1 fabrics with the zone diameter 16 times to that of TN/SA sample.

In summary, a multifunctional smart polymer coating on a commercial wipe has been successfully designed and prepared by combining the tannic acid rooted carbon dots with alginate-zinc complex. The as-formed alginate-metal aggregates possess the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics while the mussel-inspired catechol groups on entrapped TN conferred great redox-activities to the polymer coatings that are able to generate reactive free radicals (ROS) in the presence of O2, resulting a synergistic enhanced fluorescent and antibacterial performance. Moreover, this smart coating was able to produce on diverse substrates and a commercial wipe has been chosen as the fabric substrate towards healthcare applications. The coating modified smart wipes showed excellent sensing abilities to the biological species such as H2O2 and Fe3+, and sustainable antibacterial properties. Finally, taking consideration the characteristics of the nano-redox carbon dots and nontraditional AIE polysaccharides, this study is able to provide new insight into the construction of advanced fluorescent materials for biomedical uses such as promising antibacterial agents, bioimaging and biosensing fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by financial support of National Natural Science Foundation (12205225). We thank the “Educational Commission of Hubei Province” and “Wuhan Engineering Technology Research Center for Advanced Fibers” providing partial support of this project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.147402.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
